input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted as a tele‑operated platform with advanced sensor and control technology. Its hardware consists of a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and autonomy elements that together provide perception, decision‑making and actuation. The vehicle operates in a laboratory/test‑track environment for adaptive cruise control experiments, making it suitable for digital twinning because its computationally constrained ECU can off‑load functions to an external digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of ACC‑relevant data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC control algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, speed‑setpoint computation) is executed in the cloud and the resulting desired speed is sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can remotely enable/disable ACC, set the desired speed, and issue an emergency‑braking command through the UI. All services require low end‑to‑end latency communication and respect the safety‑critical nature of the system. The target user classes are operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting via data collection).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the software development kit (SDK) for low‑level hardware access, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) as the middleware for real‑time communication and component orchestration, and application modules written in Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink for algorithm development and simulation. Cloud resources are employed to host the off‑loaded ACC computation, and the UI is built on top of these middleware services to provide visualization and control capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires clock synchronization between devices with an accuracy of 100 ms, as dictated by hardware limitations; this defines the maximum permissible time drift for computation offloading and coordination between the Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin is structured in three hierarchical entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each instantiated as separate ROS‑2 packages on distinct hardware components; each entity contains one or more Functional Entities (FE) implemented as ROS‑2 nodes, establishing a single‑instance, decentralized deployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture: software components are first identified, then mapped to Functional Entities that are implemented as individual ROS‑2 packages (each containing ROS‑2 nodes). The Physical Twin Entity hosts sensor, actuator and compute resources; the Digital Twin Entity provides data management and control logic; the User Entity offers a 3D visualization and control panel UI. Coordination between entities relies on ROS‑2 communication primitives and the shared 100 ms time base.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin adopts the ISO 23247 entity‑based reference architecture, mapping software components to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS 2 packages. The Digital Twin Entity hosts data‑management and control modules, while the Physical Twin Entity contains data pre‑processing, device‑communication, and actuation functions and provides general automotive capabilities such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking. These capabilities constitute analytical/behavioral models (e.g., clustering algorithms for 3D perception and tracking algorithms for temporal association). Data originate from vehicle sensors (unspecified) that feed the Physical Twin Entity; processed data are stored via a Data Storage component. Model interactions are mediated through ROS 2 topics between the FE packages.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation functionality is encapsulated in an Actuation Functional Entity located in the Physical Twin Entity. This FE is implemented as a ROS 2 package that receives control commands from the Digital Twin Entity and forwards them to the vehicle’s physical actuators (e.g., motor drives, steering actuators). The architecture specifies the functional separation of actuation logic but does not list specific actuator types, actuation ranges, safety constraints, or addressing mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing resources are represented within the Physical Twin Entity. Raw sensor measurements are captured by Data Pre‑processing Functional Entities, each implemented as a ROS 2 package that ingests the data, performs required preprocessing (e.g., preparing point‑cloud data), and passes the processed information to higher‑level models in the Digital Twin Entity. The documents do not provide details on sensor models, sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or accuracy specifications; they only define the functional placement of sensing and preprocessing within the Physical Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the vehicle (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state such as speed and steering angle) is streamed to the Physical Twin Entity. The raw LiDAR point cloud is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (the Data Pre‑Processing FE), which transforms the point cloud into object tracks. All data streams are then passed to the Data Translation FE, which serializes the messages, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Within the Physical Twin Entity the components communicate over a ROS 2 middleware network. The resulting ACC‑relevant information (vehicle state, object tracks, projected driving path) is transmitted across the inter‑entity Wi‑Fi link (UDP transport) to the Digital Twin Entity for visualization and further processing.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Digital Twin Entity are sent back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi/UDP link. The commands include ACC enable/disable, a desired speed set‑point, and an emergency‑brake request. These commands are received by the Actuation FE in the Physical Twin Entity, which converts the desired speed into the appropriate motor voltage/actuation signal for the vehicle. The system operates in real time to meet low end‑to‑end latency requirements, but the documents do not describe explicit validation, authentication, or acknowledgement mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a hybrid network architecture. Inside each entity, software components communicate via a ROS 2 middleware network (publish/subscribe and services). Between entities, a Wi‑Fi network transports UDP packets for both sensor data (physical→virtual) and control commands (virtual→physical). Clock synchronisation across devices is required to within 100 ms accuracy to satisfy safety‑critical timing constraints. No explicit bandwidth or redundancy mechanisms are described, and security protocols are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Physical Twin Entity runs on the vehicle’s on‑board compute hardware, hosting sensor drivers and the Data Pre‑Processing and Actuation FEs as ROS 2 packages. The Digital Twin Entity is deployed on external compute resources that may reside in the cloud or at the edge, providing sufficient CPU, memory and storage to run the Data Translation, Data Collection, Reporting and 3D Visualization services. The architecture supports off‑loading of the ACC algorithm to this remote platform, enabling scalable compute and storage while maintaining low end‑to‑end latency for control loops.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture. First, key software components are enumerated (see Table I) and their relationships to sensors and actuators are defined. These components are then mapped onto Functional Entities (FEs) defined by the ISO standard. Each FE is realized as an individual ROS 2 package; a package may contain one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the FE’s functionality. The mapping yields three top‑level entities: the User Entity (hosting a User Interface FE split into a 3D Visualization component and a Control Panel component), the Digital Twin Entity (hosting data‑management and control mechanisms), and the Physical Twin Entity (hosting data pre‑processing, actuation, and device‑communication functions such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking). Each entity is deployed on a distinct hardware unit, reflecting a departure from the ISO‑defined OME but preserving the entity‑based decomposition. The development workflow includes implementation of the ROS 2 packages, integration testing on the target vehicle hardware, and verification that inter‑entity communication respects the 100 ms clock‑synchronization requirement for safety‑critical coordination. Version control is implicit in the package‑based structure, enabling independent evolution of each FE.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built to conform to the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing, specifically leveraging Part 2 (Reference Architecture) to define an entity‑based structure (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) and to allocate functionality to Functional Entities. Communication and device coordination may employ OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as suggested by the standard, though the implementation described uses ROS 2 as the middleware. The design also respects industry‑specified time‑synchronization limits (≤100 ms) for safety‑critical vehicular applications, aligning with standards that mandate stricter clock synchronization. No additional certification or compliance frameworks are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

It correctly identifies the motivation for the digital twin (offloading computation from constrained ECUs) and the system's purpose. Supported by quotes: 'physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'retrofitting it with autonomy components', 'off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction provides a comprehensive and technically precise summary of the three services defined in the text: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. It includes specific capabilities (e.g., emergency braking, path projection) and correctly identifies the target user classes (operators and developers). Supported by quotes: '1) Data Monitoring: ... UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', '2) Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', '3) Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extracted tools are precisely listed in the source document. The text specifies 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', and the programming languages 'Python 3.8... C++... and MATLAB/Simulink'. The extraction faithfully reproduces these technical details and their roles.

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the time synchronization requirements found in the source. The text explicitly states: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and notes that this is 'due to hardware limitations'. The extraction also correctly links this synchronization to the need for 'computation offloading', which is supported by the text: 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

The description correctly identifies the three hierarchical entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) and their implementation as ROS-2 packages. The source confirms this structure: 'each FE as an individual ROS2 package' and details the organization of components into the 'User Entity', 'Digital Twin Entity', and 'Physical Twin Entity'. It also supports the decentralized deployment on distinct hardware: 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

The extraction accurately describes the orchestration based on the ISO 23247 reference architecture. The source states: 'we map the SW components onto FEs and implement them... as an individual ROS2 package.' It correctly attributes the specific roles to each entity: 'Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources... and can host general automotive functionality', 'SW components that implement data management and control mechanisms are organized into the Digital Twin Entity', and 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity'. The mention of ROS-2 communication and the 100ms time base is also grounded in the text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. After reviewing the provided text, there is no information regarding integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability with other Digital Twins, or dependencies on systems outside of the described architecture (Physical, Digital, User entities and the ROS-2 network). The document focuses on the internal architecture of the specific ACC Digital Twin case study.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the ISO 23247 entity-based architecture, mapping of components to Functional Entities (FEs) as ROS2 packages, and the division of roles between Digital Twin and Physical Twin Entities. It correctly identifies data sources (vehicle sensors), the Data Storage component, and analytical models like point-cloud clustering and tracking algorithms. The details are well-supported by the text: "The standard defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality," "implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package," "Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter," and "Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database." The extraction is highly specific and grounded in the sources.

The extraction correctly identifies the Actuation Functional Entity within the Physical Twin Entity, implemented as a ROS 2 package, and its role in receiving control commands from the Digital Twin Entity to forward to physical actuators. It accurately notes the functional separation and the lack of specific technical details like actuator types or ranges in the architecture description. This is strongly supported by Table I ('Actuation FE') and Figure 2.

The extraction correctly describes the placement of sensing resources in the Physical Twin Entity and the role of Data Pre-Processing FEs in handling raw data (e.g., point-cloud preparation) for higher-level models. It accurately states the lack of specific sensor details in the documents. However, the extraction misses the specific sensor models listed in the text ('Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'), which would have made it a perfect score.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' and the source documents do not discuss model fidelity requirements, validation methods, or uncertainty quantification. The text focuses on architecture, implementation, and services without addressing fidelity or validity. Thus, the claim of absence is correct.

(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.

component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
